sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Automated scientific discovery with large language models is transforming the research lifecycle from ideation to experimentation, yet existing agents struggle to autonomously process raw data collected from scientific experiments. We introduce SciDER, a data-centric end-to-end system that automates the research lifecycle. Unlike traditional frameworks, our specialized agents collaboratively parse and analyze raw scientific data, generate hypotheses and experimental designs grounded in specific data characteristics, and write and execute corresponding code. Evaluation on three benchmarks shows SciDER excels in specialized data-driven scientific discovery and outperforms general-purpose agents and state-of-the-art models through its self-evolving memory and critic-led feedback loop. Distributed as a modular Python package, we also provide easy-to-use PyPI packages with a lightweight web interface to accelerate autonomous, data-driven research and aim to be accessible to all researchers and developers.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Automated scientific discovery with large language models is transforming the research lifecycle from ideation to experimentation, yet existing agents struggle to autonomously process raw data collected from scientific experiments. We introduce SciDER, data-centric end-to-end system that automates the research lifecycle. Unlike traditional frameworks, our specialized agents collaboratively parse and analyze raw scientific data, generate hypotheses and experimental designs grounded in specific data characteristics, and write and execute corresponding code. Evaluation on three benchmarks shows SciDER excels in specialized data-driven scientific discovery and outperforms general-purpose agents and state-of-theart models through its self-evolving memory and critic-led feedback loop. Distributed as modular Python package, we also provide easy-to-use PyPI packages with lightweight web interface to accelerate autonomous, datadriven research and aim to be accessible to all researchers and developers.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Large language models (LLMs)-based agents are catalyzing scientific discovery (Wang et al., 2024b; Garikaparthi et al., 2025; Gottweis et al., 2025). They can automate many research steps, from generating hypotheses to designing experiments, thereby speeding innovation and reducing barriers in scientific research  (Fig. 1)  . For example, AI Scientist v2 (Yamada et al., 2025) can autonomously write workshop-level papers. Despite this, creating fully independent AI research partner still faces two unique barriers. The first challenge is the limited adaptability. Most current systems are designed mainly for public machine learning datasets (Chan et al., 2025; Starace et al., 2025; Lu et al., 2024; Tang et al., 2025). Therefore, they struggle to independently analyze and process diverse real-world experimental data (Zhang et al., Figure 1: Flowchart of expert-based and LLM-based research lifecycle. 2025; Luo et al., 2026). The second challenge is the domain gap. General-purpose assistants often fail in specialized domains where abstract ideas must be translated into precise, and sometimes proprietary, experimental data formats (White et al., 2022; Tian et al., 2024; Luo et al., 2026). To address these issues, we present SciDER, Scientific Data-centric End-to-end Research system designed to flexibly automate the scientific research lifecycle. Distributed as modular Python package leveraging LangGraph (LangChain, Inc., 2026) and litellm (BerriAI, 2026), SciDER integrates ideation, data analysis, and experimentation into cohesive workflow. The system comprises four sub-agents: the ideation agent generates hypotheses and experimental plans; the data analysis agent cleans and structures data into an organized data format and corresponding analysis reports; the experimentation agent develops and executes experiment code; and the critic agent reviews all outputs to propose improvements. defining feature of our system is its self-evolving memory mechanism (Ouyang et al., 2026), which categorizes knowledge into short-term and long-term (taskspecific and project-specific) types. By retrieving relevant reasoning chunks through retrievalaugmented generation (RAG) (Lewis et al., 2020), SciDER enables test-time learning and allows the system to enhance its proficiency in general and domain-specific exploration as it accumulates taskand project-specific experience. SciDERs data-centric design for scientific discovery is one of its fundamental principles. Unlike static script-based processing from previous works, SciDER enables independent parsing and analysis of raw experimental data uploaded by users across diverse scientific domains. This data-grounded approach guarantees that knowledge of data structures, quality, and semantics informs code genIn multidisciplinary fields like physics, eration. chemistry, biology, and mathematics, where general coding assistants usually fall short in bridging the gap between abstract reasoning and executable synthesis, SciDER is therefore exceptional at solving complex research-level coding problems. Benchmarked on AI-Idea-Bench (Qiu et al., 2025), MLEBench (Chan et al., 2025), and SciCode (Tian et al., 2024), SciDER outperforms previous methods in generating ideas and conducting experiments across general and specialized domains. Human evaluation and case studies also demonstrate that SciDER effectively handles complex research tasks and enhances research capabilities. The contributions are threefold: We introduce SciDER, modular system that automates the full research lifecycle through specialized agents and an innovative self-evolving memory mechanism that supports continuous test-time memorizing and learning. We propose data-centric approach that grounds code generation of experiments in autonomous experimental analysis, enabling superior performance on interdisciplinary research problems. Extensive empirical analyses that SciDER greatly outperforms current baselines on AIIdea-Bench, MLEBench, and SciCode benchmarks, demonstrating its efficacy in managing challenging scientific reasoning and coding tasks at the research level.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Early end-to-end automated pipelines, such as The AI Scientist (Lu et al., 2024) and its follow-up, AI Scientist-v2 (Yamada et al., 2025), demonstrate that LLM-based agentic pipelines can generate hypotheses and run experiments with limited human intervention. Despite this progress, existing approaches do not provide full support for the full scientific pipeline (Tab. 1). In particular, AI researcher (Tang et al., 2025), Agent Laboratory (Schmidgall et al., 2025) ,and TinyScientist (Yu et al., 2025) focus primarily on idea generation and experiment execution without any memory mechanism. AI researcher introduces fully autonomous pipeline with Scientist-Bench for evaluating open-ended task exploration over diverse domains. TinyScientist targets usability through an interactive and controllable framework; therefore is not fully autonomous as it requires continuous user involvement. Agent Laboratory integrates literature review, experimentation, and reporting with user feedback, yet lacks an autonomous data analysis phase before experimental design. Table 1 compares SciDER with other works.

sections.title: 3 System Architecture and Workflow
sections.content: SciDER is designed as data-centric end-to-end system that flexibly automates the scientific research lifecycle. The system integrates research framework comprising ideation, data analysis, experimentation, and iterative improvement. It supports flexible inputs such as text, raw data, code, and prior papers and codebases. SciDER also offers lightweight web interface where researchers can upload their data and research topics, allowing the system to automatically create closed-loop research cycle to propose and verify new ideas. The automated research task can be formalized as follows: given datasets and research query Q, the system generates ideas (i.e., hypothesis and experiment outlines), an analysis report of data, an experiment codebase C, and results .

sections.title: 3.1 LLM-based Agents
sections.content: Ideation: Ideation(D, Q). SciDER integrated an ideation agent for automatic literature review, idea generation, and novelty assessment. The system allows users to propose an initial thought as programmable task given D. The ideation agent automatically synthesizes insights from the scientific literature and retrieves related online datasets to generate proposals tailored to the specific experimental context. Each idea proposed by the ideation agent contains (1) hypothesis about the dataset, (2) an experiment outline, and (3) Framework Functionality Extensibility Deployment Ideation Data Analysis Experiment Modular Memory Web UI Package AI Scientist (Lu et al., 2024) AI Scientist v2 (Yamada et al., 2025) AI Researcher (Tang et al., 2025) Agent Laboratory (Schmidgall et al., 2025) TinyScientist (Yu et al., 2025) DeepAnalyze (Zhang et al., 2025) SciDER (Ours) Table 1: Comparison of research agent frameworks. We evaluate existing frameworks across three dimensions: functionality, extensibility, and deployment readiness. SciDER provides research pipeline that integrates ideation, data analysis, and experimentation with test-time memory, accessible via Web UI and Python package. comparative analysis with existing work. Specifically, the ideation agent generates keywords from the input, retrieves and analyzes relevant literature from arXiv (Cornell University, 2024), Semantic Scholar (Allen Institute for AI, 2024), and PubMed (National Center for Biotechnology Information, 2024), and generates an initial hypothesis. An LLM-as-Judge evaluates these proposals for novelty across four categories: Uniqueness, Innovation, Gap Addresses, and Impact with total score of 10 (App. A.2). revision report will be generated for self-refinement (Madaan et al., 2023) to determine whether the idea proposal will be forwarded to the next agent. Data Analysis: Data(D, Q, I). SciDER allows researchers to upload experimental data from various scientific domains for automated analysis and the generation of well-organized data reports, serving as input to the experiment agent. Although the pipeline may automatically preprocess and clean the data as D, we refer to the dataset as for simplicity. This stage is the core of SciDERs data-centric paradigm, in which raw data is transformed into structured knowledge that guides subsequent research. The interface also accepts an optional user instruction to provide additional information or guide data reading and parsing. The agent iterates over the datasets folder structure, then uses tools to read and parse various file formats. Textual data is organized in formats like Parquet, JSON, and CSV, while custom readers are developed for non-standard image formats. The final data report assesses data from multiple perspectives: structure, quality, semantics, and dependency (App. A.3). Experimentation. The experimentation stage includes code generation and execution. Given the idea proposal I, the coding agent generates executable scripts tailored to the data structures and dependencies identified in the data-centric analysis report R, thereby ensuring high success rates for domain-specific tasks. The entire codebase is then transferred to the execution agent for full execution. Coding Phase: Coding(D, Q, I, R). The coding agent leverages external frameworks (i.e., OpenHands from Wang et al. and Claude Code from Anthropic) to iteratively generate executable experiment code. Coding failures (e.g., syntax and type errors) initiate reflection to rectify errors until the workspace is ready. Execution Phase: Exec(D, C). Once the coding workspace is provided with executable scripts and properly configured, the execution agent begins execution and periodically monitors progress. Typically, agents wait for executions to complete. However, if errors occur or the execution output indicates potential failure (e.g., timeout, consistently high loss, failed unit tests, or frozen screen), the execution agent terminates the running process and provides feedback to the coding agent. Critics & Feedback. After each stage of the data analysis and experimentation, critic agent will judge the performance of each stage and provide feedback for iterative reflection and self-refinement. The critic agent assesses the accuracy, completeness, and potential biases of the preceding process, identifying information gaps and logical inconsistencies (App. A.4). The agent then proposes improvements or alternative solutions to achieve balanced and comprehensive final revision. Given the n-th feedback n, the next revision output is: n+1 Ideation(F n, D, Q) ... ... n+1 Critic(F n, n+1, Rn+1, Cn+1) Figure 2: Self-evolving memory mechanism of SciDER. Memories are categorized as shortor long-term chunks within agent contexts, while new responses are summarized and integrated back into the memory bank. For simplicity, we omit the corresponding formulas for Data(), Coding(), and Exec(). 3.2 Self-evolving Memory To improve test-time performance, SciDER integrates self-evolving memory mechanism adapted from ReasoningBank (Ouyang et al., 2026) for continuous test-time learning and memorization. Unlike ReasoningBank, SciDER further organizes memory into short-term and long-term categories and categorizes long-term memory as taskand project-specific  (Fig. 2)  . Project-specific memory dynamically captures ideation and experimental results, while task-specific memory stores general guidance for similar tasks within domain. As the system runs, the memory module stores reasoning in local memory bank. For new chat sessions, relevant memory chunks are retrieved (e.g., via semantic vector or keyword search) and inserted into the context as background knowledge. Method Idea2Idea Novelty Motiv. Exp. Motiv. Exp. SCIPIP VIRSC AI-Researcher AI-Scientist SciDER 2.44 2.94 2.81 3.59 3. - 2.12 2.02 2.73 3.50 25.06 24.87 24.92 25.03 - 24.65 24.69 26.08 47.06 44. Feas. - 13.3 16.8 12.1 24.0 Table 2: Results of AI-Idea-Bench. Scores for Idea-toIdea Matching, Novelty, and Feasibility Assessment are reported. Higher values indicate better method. The feasibility score is measured at 102 magnitude.

sections.title: 4.1 Evaluation Setup
sections.content: Model settings. We use gemini-2.5-flash (Comanici et al., 2025) for idea generation in ideation assessment and as the main model for the entire experimentation evaluation pipeline. For all general and domain-specific codebase generation of experiments, we employ the Claude Code framework (Anthropic, 2025) with the Claude 4 Sonnet model. To ensure fair comparison, benchmarks for different methods use base models with similar capabilities on the same tasks. Evaluation settings. Our evaluation uses automated methods from existing benchmarks, supplemented by human feedback. To meet benchmark requirements, we evaluate agents separately(e.g., the ideation agent for ideation tasks, the coding and critic agents for general and domain-specific tasks). The self-evolving memory mechanism is disabled during benchmarking to prevent data leakage across test samples. For human evaluation, we follow the rubrics in App. A.1 and use annotators with adequate knowledge of the research lifecycle.

sections.title: 4.2 Assessment of Generated Ideas
sections.content: To rigorously evaluate the ideation phase of SciDER, we use the AI-Idea-Bench 2025 (Qiu et al., 2025) framework, which provides comprehensive and quantitative methodology for assessing AI-generated research ideas. This benchmark evaluates generated ideas using 3,495 top-tier AI papers, assessing ground-truth alignment and objective, and reference-based judgment. It establishes robust standard for measuring innovation and feasibility in automated discovery across diverse real-world scenarios with following scores: Idea-to-Idea Matching: This metric assesses conceptual similarity between generated ideas and the target paper, scoring motivation (core issue and context) and experimental design (theoretical and structural consistency) on 05 scale. Novelty Assessment: This score quantifies originality by measuring the distance between the overall idea and experiment outlines and existing literature, weighted by citation influence to yield an open-ended total. Feasibility Assessment: It evaluates the methodological grounding of an ideas motivation and experimental plan by averaging the citation influence of reference papers. Baseline results of SCIPIP (Wang et al., 2024a), VIRSC (Su et al., 2025), AI-Researcher (Tang et al., 2025), and AI-Scientist (Lu et al., 2024) are also reported from the benchmark leaderboard. Table 2 shows SciDER outperforms existing baselines in generating high-quality research ideas. Specifically, SciDER achieves breakthrough in Novelty Assessment, nearly doubling the performance of current state-of-the-art frameworks such as AIScientist and AI-Researcher, with scores of 47.06 for motivation and 44.52 for experiment matching. Furthermore, our system demonstrates improved feasibility (24.0) and stronger experiment-centric alignment (3.78 & 3.50 for Idea-to-Idea Matching). These results show that SciDER generates more innovative and executable research proposals, while staying aligned with top-tier scientific literature.

sections.title: 4.3 Performance of Experimentation
sections.content: MLE-Bench (Chan et al., 2025) is an offline benchmark based on 75 real Kaggle machine learning competitions with diverse training data sizes mirroring real-world tasks. It objectively evaluates SciDERs general capability to independently design entire experiment workflows. We compareit against three recent works: AIDE (Jiang et al., 2025) w/ o1-preview, AIRA (Toledo et al., 2025) w/ o3-preview, ML-Master (Liu et al., 2025) w/ Deepseek-R1. Table 3 compares SciDERs results against others, reporting the percentage of any medals (% Any) and gold medal (% Gold) in single pass (i.e., pass@1). SciDER achieves 7.76% higher gold medal rate than AIRA while maintaining comparable total medal rates. SciDER demonstrates comparable performance to specialized Machine Learning agents. Its research-centric design further improves scalability through iterative trial-critic-revision cycles, resulting in higher gold medal rates.

sections.title: 4.4 Capabilities of Domain-specific Tasks
sections.content: We evaluate our system on the SciCode (Tian et al., 2024) benchmark to assess its proficiency in solving complex research-level coding problems across 16 diverse subfields, including physics, chemistry, biology, mathematics, material science, and others. SciCode features 80 main problems, comprising 338 hierarchical sub-problems, which demand Method MLE-Bench (Lite) %Any %Gold AIDE (Jiang et al., 2025) ML-Master (Liu et al., 2025) AIRA (Toledo et al., 2025) SciDER (Ours) 16.90 48.50 47.73 45.45 9.40 18.10 28.64 36.40 Table 3: Results of MLE-Bench. For all methods tested on the Lite split of MLE-Bench, overall and gold medal rates are reported. models utilize deep scientific background knowledge, recall, and multi-step reasoning. Its multidisciplinary scope comprehensively evaluates systems generalization to realistic, less familiar research contexts where general-purpose coding assistants typically fall short. On the SciCode benchmark, SciDER performs noticeably better than the state-of-the-art models, as shown in Fig. 3. SciDER outperforms highly capable models such as GPT-5 (13.85% & 38.26%), achieving 15.38% & 42.71% success rate on both main problems and sub-problems. These findings demonstrate that our iterative feedback mechanisms and agentic architecture effectively execute code synthesis guided by scientific reasoning. Figure 3: Results of SciCode. Solve rates for main and sub-problems are reported, with higher rates indicating greater capability for domain-specific problems.

sections.title: 4.5 Case Study
sections.content: We present case study demonstrating the usefulness and effectiveness of SciDER and its potential to automate the traditional data science workflow in the natural sciences domain. In this case study, an astrophysicist with little machine learning experience can use SciDER to develop fully functional Figure 4: screenshot of the web interface of SciDER. ML pipeline for astrophysics tasks. The astrophysicist aims to use the Kepler Exoplanet Dataset (Akeson et al., 2013) to determine whether candidate signal is an exoplanet from noisy time-series data. The astrophysicist first selects the Full Workflow and inputs query  (Fig. 4)  : Use the Kepler Exoplanet Dataset to train and iteratively refine set of machine learning models that detect exoplanet transit signals from stellar light curves, autonomously generating and testing feature hypotheses (e.g., periodic dips, depth, duration, signal-to-noise ratio) to maximize classification performance. After finishing the configuration, the astrophysicist clicks on Run the full workflow to launch the workflow. After SciDER completed the final steps, it generated report. SciDER conducted comparative experiment among three distinct models with feature engineering and achieved an 98% F1 score, demonstrating that SciDER can engineer machine learning pipelines that achieve substantial performance. The system also generates detailed report and allows users to review each agents actions through the UI in Fig. 5. Through the case study, we demonstrate how domain experts can use SciDER to build high-performance machine learning pipelines for domain-specific tasks with minimal interventions and auditing the working process SciDER with minimal effort. The presented case study can be viewed in the demos Browser mode. Figure 5: screenshot of the Accordion UI to check the output of each sub-agents output step-by-step. 4.6 Human Evaluation To evaluate the practical utility of SciDER as an autonomous research assistant, we conducted human evaluation study involving 13 participants with domain expertise (including Ph.D. students, professors, and industrial researchers) in the scientific research lifecycle, finalized at the time of submission. Annotators were asked to rate their experience based on the "helpfulness" rubric detailed in App. A.1, which assesses dimensions such as workflow reduction, data-grounded accuracy, and code executability. The system achieved mean score of 4.846/5.000 with low variance of 0.376.

sections.title: 5 Conclusion
sections.content: In this work, we present SciDER, useful end-toend system that automates the scientific research lifecycle. By combining ideation, data analysis, and iterative experimentation with self-evolving memory mechanism, SciDER bridges the gap between abstract scientific reasoning, data-centric insights, and executable code synthesis. SciDER outperforms earlier efforts on idea-generation judgment, general machine-learning benchmarks, and multidisciplinary scientific coding tasks. We provide lightweight web interface and distribute SciDER as modular Python package to ensure accessibility, helping researchers to initiate complete, closed-loop research cycle with single click. We believe that SciDER will reduce the technical barriers for researchers, ultimately improving research effectiveness and promoting the broader adoption of autonomous research agents.

sections.title: Ethical Considerations
sections.content: Although SciDER is intended to automate and expedite the scientific research lifecycle, its implementation entails ethical obligations. The system is prone to hallucinations or logical inconsistencies because it depends on LLMs for core reasoning and code synthesis. This could lead to incorrect hypotheses, biased experiments, and hallucinated results. Data privacy is also at risk when processing tasks rely on external LLM APIs; misconfigurations or operations may inadvertently upload private or sensitive experimental data to unaffiliated platforms. In addition to preventing potential copyright violations, automated collection of literature and data from online repositories must be conducted carefully. Instead of completely replacing human expertise, SciDER should be used as collaborative assistant to reduce these risks. To ensure scientific integrity and legal compliance, it is recommended that researchers manually validate all AI-generated reports, code, and findings.

sections.title: A Appendix
sections.content: A.1 Human Evaluation To evaluate the practical utility of SciDER as an autonomous research assistant, we define specialized helpfulness rubric. This metric assesses how effectively the system reduces the human researchers workload across the research lifecycle, from data parsing to experimental execution. Score 1 Very Poor (Obstacle): The system fails to provide any useful output. It either generates incoherent research ideas, fails to parse the provided raw data, or produces non-executable code. Human intervention is required to restart the entire process from scratch. Score 2 Poor (Low Utility): The system provides generic or shallow insights that do not reflect the specific characteristics of the experimental data. Despite generating some output, the design or code requires significant manual correction to function. Score 3 Average (Moderate Assistance): The system successfully automates routine tasks, such as basic data profiling and literature keyword searching. The experimental plan is feasible but lacks depth or novelty. It functions as baseline assistant but requires moderate human guidance and revision. Score 4 Good (Significant Assistance): The system demonstrates clear understanding of It generates wellthe data-centric paradigm. structured data reports and executable scripts with only minor gaps. The critic agent provides relevant feedback that noticeably improves the research quality. It effectively saves the researcher time in the exploration phase. Score 5 Excellent (Autonomous Collaborator): The system acts as high-level reIt provides deep sciensearch collaborator. tific insights grounded in the raw data, identifies non-obvious patterns, and generates highfidelity code/reports that are ready for immediate use. The self-evolving memory effectively utilizes project-specific context to refine subsequent steps without human input. A.

sections.title: Ideation Criteria
sections.content: The ideation evaluates the idea proposals based on the following criteria: Uniqueness: This criterion measures the degree of differentiation from existing work, assigned score of 03 points, ranging from simple replication to the introduction of entirely new concepts. Innovation: This assesses the originality of the methodology or theoretical framework, with score of 03 reflecting the transition from standard methods to the proposal of novel approaches. Gap Addressing: This evaluates how effectively the proposal tackles identified research gaps or limitations, with score of 02 based on the significance of the gap being filled. Impact: This quantifies the potential contribution of the research to the field, ranging from 02 points from minimal expected contribution to high-impact potential. A.3 Data Analysis Criteria The data analysis agent assesses the data by: Structure: Evaluates file formats, data types, and organizational patterns like schemas and encodings to define the datas technical framework. Quality: Analyzes data integrity by identifying missing values, outliers, and inconsistencies through sample inspection and summaries. Semantics: Interprets the domain-specific meaning and logical significance of data fields to ensure the information is correctly understood. Dependency: Maps relationships between data entities, such as foreign keys and file links, to establish cross-references and the required processing sequence. A.4 Critic Criteria We present the rules for the critic agent to evaluate and provide feedback for each sub-agent: Accuracy: Verify that all factual claims, data points, and references are precise and verified against reliable sources. Completeness: Ensure the response addresses every component of the prompt and satisfies all user requirements without omission. Bias: Identify and eliminate any subjective leanings, cultural prejudices, or non-neutral viewpoints in the content. Information Gap: Specify any missing context, depth, or technical details required for comprehensive understanding. Misinformation: Detect any logical hallucinations, fabricated facts, or misleading statements. Suggested Improvement: Provide clear, actionable instructions to correct the identified flaws and enhance the output quality. Figure 6: Left: The research proposal for this iteration, with the green highlights indicating the core experimental topic. Right: The experimental outputs, including the evaluation report (red highlight) and the corresponding implementation code (lower right). A.5 User Interface To further enhance the usability of the SciDER package, we develop lightweight and easy-to-use web interface that enables users to interact directly with SciDER  (Fig. 4)  . The interface comprises three panels: workflow selection panel, workflow launch panel, and chat panel. Users can select workflow (ideation, data analysis, experiment, or full), input queries and datasets in the launch panel, and review or modify results via the chat panel. Custom datasets can be uploaded to the workspace filesystem for analysis by SciDER to enable data-driven pipeline. An example output of SciDER is shown in Fig. 6, which includes research proposal, an experimental report, and the corresponding code implementation. Users can track the workflow in step-by-step manner via an Accordion interface  (Fig. 5)  , composed of multiple collapsible panels that improve the transparency of SciDER execution process.

sections.title: B SciDER Python Package
sections.content: SciDER is distributed as Python package under the Apache 2.0 License, leveraging LangGraph and litellm for easy and modular use. As Algorithm 1 illustrates, the package can execute the full research workflow in just few lines of code. The full workflow can also be divided into sub-workflows, which are included in the scider.workflows package.

sections.title: C Limitations
sections.content: While SciDER demonstrates capabilities in automating the research lifecycle, there are several Algorithm 1 SciDER Python example usage from scider.default.models import (cid:44) from scider.workflows import run_full_workflow register_gemini_medium_high_models # 1. Register the models you want to use register_gemini_medium_high_models() # 2. Run the full workflow wf = run_full_workflow( data_path="/path/to/data/", workspace_path="/path/to/workspace/", user_query="Discover insights about RAG", ) # 3. The final state after the workflow print(wf.final_summary) limitations that we aim to address in future work. First, the current system lacks module for the automated generation of scientific papers. This design choice stems from our primary focus on the more critical challenges of data-centric research: autonomous data analysis, experimental planning, and the synthesis of executable code. Given that prior work has established the feasibility of automated manuscript writing, we consider the perfection of the experimental lifecycle to be of higher priority for data-centric agent. We intend to include paper-writing functionality in subsequent iterations of the system. Second, SciDER is currently dependent on external LLM APIs for its core reasoning and experimentation tasks. This reliance introduces potential constraints regarding operational costs, API availability, and data privacy when processing sensitive or proprietary experimental datasets.
